text: This operation is a robotic assisted splenectomy for a symptomatic, nineteen centimeter splenic cyst. Prior to the operation, this patient received the Haemophilus influenzae B, the pneumococcus, and the meningococcus vaccination.
The patient is positioned in a lazy right lateral decubitus position on the operating table, preparing for the robotic-assisted splenectomy.

The surgeon begins the operation by mobilizing the splenic flexure and transecting the splenocolic ligament to prevent tension on the spleen. The lesser sac is entered, and a counterclockwise dissection using a vessel sealer transects the gastrosplenic ligament. The short gastric vessels are safely transected with the vessel sealer. After mobilizing the greater curve of the stomach, the splenic hilum is exposed, which has been brought to a more anterior location by the expanding splenic cyst.

The surgeon attempts to release the lateral splenophrenic ligament to expose the posterior aspect of the spleen, aiming to access the anterior and posterior splenic hilum. Due to the bulky spleen caused by the splenic cyst, the surgeon shifts focus to the inferior hilum.

The surgeon uses a white load vascular stapler to transect a portion of the splenic hilum, with the splenic artery visible traveling along the superior aspect of the pancreas and branching into upper and lower polar arteries. The surgeon is prepared with a grasper to control any bleeding, while the assistant has a proline suture ready for ligating larger vessels if necessary.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to transect lighter attachments near the tail of the pancreas, avoiding injury to the pancreatic tail. The splenic artery is carefully skeletonized to prevent damage from the bulky stapler. Progressive piecemeal dissection reveals additional arterial branches leading to the splenic hilum, which are isolated with precision.

The surgeon returns to the inferior aspect of the splenic hilum, revealing the posterior border of the spleen. The final pedicle of the hilum is visible, with concerns about the proximity of the pancreatic tail. The surgeon uses a stapler with a lateral twist to clear denser tissue. After transecting the hilum, attempts are made to release the lateral and superior attachments of the spleen. Due to inflammation caused by the expanding cyst, the spleen remains cumbersome, prompting the surgeon to drain the cyst for better access to the remaining splenic ligaments.

The surgeon drains two liters of fluid from the splenic cyst, allowing better access to the remaining attachments. Dense inflammatory adhesions are visible near the diaphragm, and the surgeon uses blunt dissection and a vessel sealer to carefully separate these tissues, avoiding electrocautery to prevent potential injury to the diaphragm.

The surgeon marks the abdominal midline with a pin while the patient is in the supine position, then transitions them to the lateral decubitus position. A three-centimeter extraction incision is made in the supraumbilical area, with the midline and linea alba clearly marked for easier identification. The spleen is removed using morselization with ring forceps and finger fracture. A 45mm and 60mm stapler are available for stapling the splenic hilum, with the smaller stapler useful for sequential transections.

The surgeon irrigates the splenic bed and evaluates it for further bleeding after the spleen is completely freed from attachments. A 15mm endocatch bag is advanced through the extended three-centimeter incision in the abdominal midline for spleen removal, which is morcellated. Consideration is given to leaving a surgical drain if there is concern about continued bleeding, seroma, or pancreatic leak.
